name: Java Collections Framework Hierarchy
chatResponse: I will walk you through the structural hierarchy of the Java Collections Framework and explain how the different interfaces and classes relate to one another.

steps.title: The Framework Foundation
steps.content: The **Java Collections Framework (JCF)** is a set of classes and interfaces that implement commonly reusable collection data structures. It provides a unified architecture for storing and manipulating a group of objects. At the top level, it is divided into two distinct branches: the `Collection` hierarchy and the `Map` hierarchy.

steps.title: The Collection Interface Branch
steps.content:
The `Collection` interface is the root of most collection types. It has three main sub-interfaces:
- **List**: An ordered collection that allows duplicate elements (e.g., `ArrayList`, `LinkedList`).
- **Set**: A collection that cannot contain duplicate elements (e.g., `HashSet`, `TreeSet`).
- **Queue**: Designed for holding elements prior to processing, typically in FIFO (First-In-First-Out) order.

steps.title: The Map Interface Branch
steps.content:
Unlike the others, the `Map` interface does *not* inherit from `Collection`. Instead, it maps unique keys to values. Common implementations include:
- **HashMap**: Provides constant-time performance for basic operations but does not guarantee order.
- **TreeMap**: Stores elements in a sorted order based on the keys' natural ordering or a custom comparator.

steps.title: Concrete Implementations
steps.content: While interfaces define the behavior, **Concrete Classes** provide the actual logic. For example, `ArrayList` uses a dynamic array internally, whereas `LinkedList` uses a doubly-linked list. Choosing the right implementation depends on your performance requirements for adding, removing, and searching for elements.

visualCode:
graph TD
  subgraph CollectionsBranch [Collection Hierarchy]
    Collection["Collection (Interface)"]
    List["List (Interface)"]
    Set["Set (Interface)"]
    Queue["Queue (Interface)"]
    
    Collection --> List
    Collection --> Set
    Collection --> Queue
    
    List --> ArrayList[ArrayList]
    List --> LinkedList[LinkedList]
    
    Set --> HashSet[HashSet]
    Set --> TreeSet[TreeSet]
    
    Queue --> PriorityQueue[PriorityQueue]
    Queue --> Deque["Deque (Interface)"]
  end

  subgraph MapBranch [Map Hierarchy]
    Map["Map (Interface)"]
    Map --> HashMap[HashMap]
    Map --> TreeMap[TreeMap]
    Map --> LinkedHashMap[LinkedHashMap]
  end
